---
title: "Scaling Laws After the Hype"
subtitle: "What Practitioners Should Know About the Economics, Limits, and Current Shape of Modern AI"
description: "A practitioner-oriented explanation of scaling laws: what they are, how they shaped the history of machine learning, why they changed the economics of AI, and how their meaning has changed after Chinchilla, post-training, inference-time compute, and enterprise deployment constraints."
author: "Antonio Montano"
date: today
categories: [essay, machine learning]
draft: true
---

## Introduction

Scaling laws are among the few ideas in modern artificial intelligence that changed both the science and the industrial organization of the field. They did not merely say that larger models perform better. That had already been observed many times, in different forms, across machine learning history. Their stronger claim was that improvement could often be described by regular empirical curves: when model size, data, and compute increase, loss tends to fall in a relatively predictable way, frequently close to a power law over a large range of scales.[^kaplan-scaling-laws]

That observation converted a vague engineering intuition into something closer to capital planning. If loss decreases smoothly with scale, then model development is no longer only a matter of clever architectures, handcrafted features, or local experimentation. It becomes a resource-allocation problem. Given a fixed budget, how much should be spent on parameters, tokens, training compute, data quality, post-training, inference-time computation, and serving infrastructure? This is why scaling laws matter to practitioners. They are not mystical laws of intelligence. They are empirical cost curves for learning systems.

The first-principles structure is simple. A machine-learning model is an optimization system. It receives data, adjusts parameters, and minimizes some loss function. If the model has too little capacity, it cannot represent the relevant structure. If the dataset is too small or too poor, the model cannot infer the structure reliably. If the training compute is insufficient, optimization does not move the parameters far enough toward a useful solution. Scaling laws arise when these constraints relax in a regular way: more capacity, more data, and more computation reduce loss, but with diminishing marginal returns. Each additional order of magnitude buys improvement, yet usually less improvement than the previous order of magnitude.

This is the operational meaning of a power law. It does not say that progress is free. It says the opposite: progress can be bought, but the price increases sharply. A system may continue to improve as compute grows, while the economic question becomes whether the next improvement is worth the next unit of capital, energy, latency, engineering complexity, and operational risk.

Historically, this gave quantitative form to what Richard Sutton called the **bitter lesson**: over the long run, general methods that can exploit computation tend to beat methods that rely primarily on human-designed domain structure.[^sutton-bitter-lesson] Scaling laws made that lesson measurable in language modeling. They helped justify the move from narrow task-specific models toward foundation models trained on broad corpora and then adapted through prompting, fine-tuning, retrieval, tools, and workflow integration.

The GPT-3 moment made the industrial consequence visible. Scaling did not only reduce language-model loss; it also improved task-agnostic few-shot behavior, allowing a single model to perform many tasks from natural-language instructions and a small number of examples, without task-specific gradient updates.[^gpt3-few-shot] This shifted enterprise AI from the classical question, “Which model should we train for this task?”, to a more architectural question: “Which general model, adaptation pattern, data layer, governance mechanism, and cost-control regime should we assemble for this business process?”

The first wave of scaling-law thinking, however, also produced a misleading simplification. The early lesson was often interpreted as “make the model bigger.” The Chinchilla result corrected that reading. Hoffmann and colleagues showed that many large language models were undertrained: they had too many parameters relative to the number of tokens used during training. Under their dense-transformer experimental regime, compute-optimal training required model size and training tokens to scale roughly together.[^chinchilla] The lesson for practitioners is important: the largest model is not necessarily the economically best model. A smaller model trained on more and better data may outperform a larger but undertrained model, while also being cheaper to serve.

This article takes scaling laws seriously, but not literally. They are not laws of physics. They are empirical regularities conditioned by architecture, optimizer, data distribution, tokenization, loss definition, compute accounting, and scale range. Their value is not that they predict the future with certainty. Their value is that they discipline the conversation. They force practitioners to ask where the bottleneck actually is.

For enterprise adoption, that distinction is decisive. Most organizations do not need to reproduce frontier pretraining. They need to decide when to use a frontier model, when to route to a smaller model, when to improve retrieval, when to fine-tune, when to invest in domain data, when to use inference-time reasoning, and when the right answer is not a better model but a better process boundary. Scaling laws are useful only when translated into these economic and architectural choices.

The central argument of this article is therefore pragmatic: scaling laws shaped the history of modern AI because they made performance improvement look predictable enough to finance. Their current relevance is different. They now help practitioners reason about the marginal return of different forms of scale: training scale, data scale, post-training scale, inference-time scale, and deployment scale. The mature lesson is not “always scale.” It is: identify the constraint, measure the return, and scale the variable that actually changes the business outcome.

[^kaplan-scaling-laws]: Jared Kaplan et al., **“Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models,”** *arXiv*, 2020. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.08361)

[^sutton-bitter-lesson]: Richard S. Sutton, **“The Bitter Lesson,”** *Incomplete Ideas*, 2019. [URL](https://www.incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson.html)

[^gpt3-few-shot]: Tom B. Brown et al., **“Language Models are Few-Shot Learners,”** *arXiv*, 2020. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165)

[^chinchilla]: Jordan Hoffmann et al., **“Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,”** *arXiv*, 2022. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556)

From this section onward I use footnote ids in the requested form. I would also rename the previous introduction footnotes as `[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-001]`, `[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-002]`, etc., to keep the article consistent. The section below relies mainly on Hestness et al. for early cross-domain empirical scaling, Kaplan et al. for language-model scaling, and Hoffmann et al. for compute-optimal allocation. ([arXiv][1])

## What Scaling Laws Are

A scaling law is an empirical relationship between the resources used to train a model and the performance obtained from that model. In the usual language-model setting, the relevant resources are model size, training data, and compute. Model size is usually measured in parameters. Training data is usually measured in tokens. Compute is usually measured in floating-point operations, although in real deployments the more concrete variables are GPU hours, accelerator availability, memory bandwidth, energy, latency, and cost.

The object being measured is normally not “intelligence” but loss. For language models, this is often cross-entropy loss: the penalty assigned when the model predicts the next token poorly. Lower loss means that, on average, the model assigns higher probability to the actual continuation of text. This does not automatically imply that the model is safer, more truthful, more useful, or more aligned with a business process. It means that the model has become better at the statistical prediction problem used during training.

The first important point for practitioners is therefore negative: scaling laws do not directly measure business value. They measure improvement on a training or evaluation objective. Business value appears only after an additional mapping: from loss to task performance, from task performance to workflow reliability, and from workflow reliability to economic impact. That mapping can be strong in some cases, weak in others, and discontinuous in many enterprise processes.

The generic form is simple. Suppose that a model has $N$ parameters and is trained on $D$ tokens. The loss can often be approximated as the sum of three terms:

$$
L(N,D) \approx L_{\infty} + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}}
$$

Here, $L_{\infty}$ is the irreducible loss under the given data distribution and modeling setup. The term $\frac{A}{N^{\alpha}}$ represents the penalty from limited model capacity. The term $\frac{B}{D^{\beta}}$ represents the penalty from limited data. The constants $A$, $B$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$ are not universal constants. They are fitted from experiments. They depend on the architecture, optimizer, data distribution, tokenization, training procedure, and scale range.

This equation is useful not because it is metaphysically deep, but because it expresses a hard economic fact: improvement has diminishing returns. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive but smaller than one, each multiplication of parameters or data reduces loss, but each additional multiplication buys less absolute improvement than the previous one. This is the practical meaning of the power law. It permits continued progress while also making progress increasingly expensive.

Early empirical work found this type of regularity across multiple deep-learning domains, including machine translation, language modeling, image processing, and speech recognition.[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-001] Kaplan and colleagues then made the result especially influential for neural language models by showing that loss scaled predictably with model size, dataset size, and training compute across several orders of magnitude.[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-002] The decisive shift was not merely technical. Once performance appeared predictable, large-scale model training became easier to finance, plan, and industrialize.

A second equation captures the compute constraint. For dense transformer pretraining, training compute is roughly proportional to the product of model size and the number of training tokens:

$$
C \propto N \cdot D
$$

This is only an approximation. Exact compute depends on architecture, sequence length, attention implementation, optimizer, hardware utilization, sparsity, activation checkpointing, and other engineering details. But the approximation is sufficient to expose the central trade-off. With a fixed compute budget, increasing $N$ usually means decreasing $D$, unless the total budget also grows. A practitioner cannot escape this constraint by rhetoric. A larger model trained on too few tokens can be undertrained. A smaller model trained on more tokens can be more efficient.

This is why the term “compute-optimal” matters. A compute-optimal model is not the largest model one can train. It is the model that gives the lowest loss for a given compute budget. The distinction is basic but often ignored. If two models consume the same training compute, and the smaller model trained on more data reaches lower loss, then the larger model was not compute-optimal, even if it looked more impressive in parameter count.

The Chinchilla result made this point explicit. Hoffmann and colleagues trained many models across different parameter counts and token budgets and found that, in their dense-transformer regime, compute-optimal training required model size and training tokens to scale roughly together. Their conclusion contradicted the simplified industry reading of early scaling-law work, where the apparent lesson was to scale parameters aggressively while keeping training data comparatively limited.[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-003]

For practitioners, this correction has a direct interpretation. Parameters are not capability by themselves. Parameters are capacity. Capacity becomes useful only when trained with enough relevant data and enough optimization compute. Buying, renting, or deploying a larger model may therefore be a poor economic decision if the task does not require that capacity, if latency matters, if inference volume is high, or if a smaller model can be adapted to the domain more cheaply.

The third useful distinction is between three kinds of scale.

First, there is **model scale**: the number of trainable parameters and the architecture that organizes them. Larger models can represent more complex functions and often learn more sample-efficiently, but they are more expensive to train and serve.

Second, there is **data scale**: the number and quality of training examples. In language models this is usually tokens, but the raw count is not enough. Deduplication, diversity, domain relevance, legal usability, contamination control, and signal-to-noise ratio matter. More data is useful only if it adds learnable structure rather than noise, leakage, or repetition.

Third, there is **compute scale**: the amount of optimization performed. Compute connects model and data. For dense pretraining, it is the budget that must be allocated between capacity and exposure to data. For deployment, compute also appears again as inference-time cost: the cost of generating answers, using longer context, sampling multiple candidates, calling tools, verifying outputs, or running reasoning models.

These three scales should not be collapsed into one word: “bigger.” A bigger model, a bigger dataset, and a bigger compute budget are different interventions. They have different bottlenecks, different cost structures, and different operational consequences. Treating them as one thing is one of the main reasons scaling-law discussions become misleading outside research labs.

A useful practitioner translation is the following.

| Technical variable | What it means physically             | What it means economically                                   | Typical practitioner mistake                                                    |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Parameters         | Stored adjustable degrees of freedom | Capacity and serving cost                                    | Assuming more parameters automatically mean better business performance         |
| Tokens / examples  | Exposure to data during training     | Data acquisition, cleaning, rights, and governance           | Counting data volume while ignoring quality and relevance                       |
| Training compute   | Optimization work                    | Capital expenditure, accelerator time, energy, schedule risk | Treating training as the main cost while ignoring deployment                    |
| Loss               | Prediction error on an objective     | Weak proxy for capability                                    | Confusing lower loss with safer or more reliable workflow behavior              |
| Inference compute  | Work done at answer time             | Marginal cost per use, latency, throughput                   | Using expensive reasoning where a cheaper model or retrieval step is sufficient |

The most important consequence is that scaling laws are marginal-return tools. They help answer a constrained question: if one more unit of investment is available, where should it go? More parameters, more data, better data, longer training, better post-training, better retrieval, more inference-time reasoning, or better process integration?

In enterprise AI, this question is usually more valuable than asking which public model is strongest in the abstract. A model can be globally weaker and locally superior if it is cheaper, faster, easier to govern, easier to deploy on private infrastructure, or better adapted to a narrow domain. Conversely, a frontier model can be technically stronger but economically inferior for high-volume, low-margin, latency-sensitive tasks.

The first-principles conclusion is therefore compact. A scaling law is not a prophecy about artificial general intelligence. It is a fitted relationship between resources and error. It becomes useful for practitioners only when translated into constraints: budget, latency, volume, data rights, reliability, observability, security, and process impact. Without that translation, scaling laws become a slogan. With it, they become one of the most powerful tools for reasoning about the economics of AI systems.

[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-001]: Joel Hestness et al., **“Deep Learning Scaling is Predictable, Empirically,”** *arXiv*, 2017. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.00409)

[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-002]: Jared Kaplan et al., **“Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models,”** *arXiv*, 2020. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.08361)

[^scaling-laws-after-the-hype-003]: Jordan Hoffmann et al., **“Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models,”** *arXiv*, 2022. [URL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556)

The next section should be **“How Scaling Laws Changed the History of Machine Learning”**, because it can connect the technical idea to ImageNet, transformers, GPT-3, foundation models, Chinchilla, and the industrialization of frontier AI.

[1]: https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.00409?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Deep Learning Scaling is Predictable, Empirically"
